Text: One of the most rewarding parts of today was when I new student was able to say the first letter of her name, "D". The teacher told me that this child was thus far unable to say her name or the first letter of her name due to a speech impediment. (This session was my first time working with the student.) I was doing an exercise with the kids where we said our name, the first letter of our name, and our favorite food that starts with the same letter. When it was this child's turn, the teacher informed me she wouldn't be able to say the "D". The child next to her also has a name that starts with the letter "D", so I repeated the first child's name and that her name starts with a "D", and then I said "also, D" referring to the child. She repeated back to me "also D". It felt like a huge success!
label: Phonics Development: One of the most rewarding parts of today was when I new student was able to say the first letter of her name, "D". The t...

Text: Yes, I wrote out an abbreviated version of what my lesson plan would be, based on what we have covered the previous two-weeks, and then I adjusted according to each class. This is a condensed look at my lesson plan (which also had variation in itself): LESSON OVERVIEW: In Lesson 8 Week 9 Students practice and perform Movement Sentences with transitions - one dance to an audience. Students reflect on their dance experience by writing a thank you note to Oge Mora! Tools Word Cards Chop, Shake, Stir (written) Curvy, Leap, Walk, Skip, Gallop Wide, Narrow High, Middle, Low Round Straight Jump Above, Below, In Front, Behind, Under, Forward, Backward, Around Meet, Dance, Part Supplies Worksheets Pencils Crayons Spots Scarves Chart Paper Blue Tape Warm-Up (5 Minutes) Head to Toe Stew - Broth Chop - Food for the Stew Shake - Salt and Pepper Exploration (13 Minutes) Three Groups - Three Sentences Sentence - Transition - Sentence - Transition - Sentence Ending Section Together Community Stew! Take a Bow!

Text: All of the students have explored the three movement sentences. For the culminating dance, I split the classes into 3 groups, before they merge into 1 big group. For some of the classes, we have rotated the groups so they are taking turns in the different groups (each group is one of three movement sentences). In one class we have reached the part of the dance where the whole group comes together to do all three movement sentences, using space in all areas of our "stage". The students have also selected the transitions from one sentence to the next

Text: I focused on talking about community, pot luck dinners, connections with food, the great things we each add to every setting we are in, and the ways we can share dance and energy! Regarding the lesson plan..... 1.) I asked the kids what they remembered from the book (who had recently come to Omu for the stew?), 2.) Finished reading the book 3.) Had the kids point out something they noticed on the thank you card page of the book 4.) Talked about community and our special qualities 5.) Had volunteers (in some classes) write Meet, Dance, Part 6.) Made 2 Straight Lines 7.) Meet - say hi to your partner, then Dance!, then Part 8.) Talked about the word part and used the example of a part in our hair! 9.) Meet (come together), Dance, Part 10.) Asked who remembered our vocabulary from 2 weeks ago 11.) Used 2 words - Walk and Meet, Dance and Freeze, Walk and Part, etc. 12.) Cool Down using Wide, Narrow, Round, Breathing in the imaginary aroma of the stew!
label: Student Centered: I focused on talking about community, pot luck dinners, connections with food, the great things we each add to every set...
